Section: Section 1: CreateGeometryType.sql - Define the geometry type
Note: Composite type `(lon double precision[], lat double precision[])`. A point is a single-element array. A polygon is 3+ vertices. No explicit closing required.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS geometry supports 2D/3D/4D (Z/M), MultiPoint/MultiLineString/MultiPolygon/GeometryCollection, polygons with holes (inner rings), embedded SRID. YB: 2D only, single simple polygon, no holes, no SRID field.
In Plain English: The fundamental 2D flat-map shape. Stored as two arrays of numbers (X and Y).

Function name (including params): ST_MakePoint(p_lon double precision, p_lat double precision)
Section: Section 1: CreateGeometryType.sql - Define the geometry type
Note: Creates a single-point geometry.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS geometry supports 3D/4D coordinates (Z/M). YB: 2D only, no capacity for Z/M in the underlying arrays.
In Plain English: Draw a dot at this exact coordinate.

Function name (including params): ST_MakePolygon(p_lon double precision[], p_lat double precision[])
Section: Section 1: CreateGeometryType.sql - Define the geometry type
Note: Creates a polygon from parallel vertex arrays.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS accepts shell + optional array of hole LineStrings, supports 3D/M. YB: no holes (inner rings) support, 2D only.
In Plain English: Draw a shape using this list of connected coordinates.

Function name (including params): ST_MakeEnvelope(p_lon_min double precision, p_lat_min double precision, p_lon_max double precision, p_lat_max double precision)
Section: Section 1: CreateGeometryType.sql - Define the geometry type
Note: Creates a rectangle geometry from bounding box corners.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS supports 3D bounding boxes and tracks SRID status. YB: 2D only, no SRID field.
Can we support it: Possible, by building a C-extension geometry type. A proper GSERIALIZED struct supports Z/M dimensions and embeds the SRID metadata.
In Plain English: Draw a rectangle using these minimum and maximum coordinates.

Function name (including params): ST_MakeEnvelope(p_lon_min double precision, p_lat_min double precision, p_lon_max double precision, p_lat_max double precision, p_srid integer)
Section: Section 1: CreateGeometryType.sql - Define the geometry type
Note: Creates a rectangle geometry from bounding box corners.
PostGIS Gaps: Accepts the SRID parameter but silently ignores it, delegating to the 2D-only 4-arg version.
Can we support it: Possible, by building a C-extension geometry type. The C constructor will write the provided SRID integer directly into the geometry header byte 5-8 of the GSERIALIZED header.
In Plain English: Draw a rectangle using these minimum and maximum coordinates.

Section: Section 1: CreateGeometryType.sql - Define the geometry type
Note:
Encodes geometry to OGC Well-Known Binary (little-endian). Handles POINT, LINESTRING, POLYGON, and empty geometries. 

So basically transfer YB language to Geoserver language
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS encodes all OGC types (Multi*, GeometryCollection, 3D). YB: Point/LineString/Polygon only, no multi-geometry, no 3D.
In Plain English: Convert this shape into raw bytes (WKB) for software like GeoServer to read.

Section: Section 1: CreateGeometryType.sql - Define the geometry type
Note:
Converts a 64-bit float to 8 little-endian bytes. Uses `float8send()` then reverses byte order.

C0 5B F8 FC 7A E1 47 AE -> AE 47 E1 7A FC F8 5B C0
In Plain English: Converts a decimal number into raw bytes so GeoServer can read it.

Section: Section 1: CreateGeometryType.sql - Define the geometry type
Note:
Encodes an unsigned integer as a protobuf-style variable-length integer.

lm__twkb_uvarint(5)     →  '\x05'::bytea          (1 byte)
lm__twkb_uvarint(300)   →  '\xac02'::bytea        (2 bytes)
In Plain English: Compresses a positive number into a tiny byte format for fast map rendering.

Section: Section 1: CreateGeometryType.sql - Define the geometry type
Note:
Zigzag-encodes a signed integer, then varint-encodes it. 


lm__twkb_svarint(1)     →  '\x02'::bytea          (1 byte)   -- zigzag: 1→2, then varint
lm__twkb_svarint(-1)    →  '\x01'::bytea          (1 byte)   -- zigzag: -1→1, then varint
lm__twkb_svarint(-50)   →  '\x63'::bytea          (1 byte)   -- zigzag: -50→99, then varint
In Plain English: Compresses a positive or negative number into a tiny byte format.

Section: Section 1: CreateGeometryType.sql - Define the geometry type
Note: Tiny WKB. Delta-compressed coordinates with protobuf-style varint encoding. Much smaller than WKB.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS encodes all OGC types with precision control and bbox/size/idlist options. YB: Point/LineString/Polygon only, no multi-geometry, no bbox/size/idlist metadata.
In Plain English: Compress this shape into tiny raw bytes (TWKB) for extremely fast map rendering.

Section: Section 3: CreateGeographyType.sql - Define the geography type
Note: Geodesic distance in meters on the WGS84 ellipsoid. Iterative Vincenty formula. Sub-millimeter accuracy. Falls back to Haversine for near-antipodal points.
In Plain English: Calculates the highly exact, surveyor-grade distance between two points on the Earth.

Section: Section 3: CreateGeographyType.sql - Define the geography type
Note: Haversine great-circle distance in meters. Same thing as above but different type
PostGIS Gaps:
PostGIS geography ST_Distance handles all geometry types (polygon-to-polygon, line-to-polygon, etc.) using geodesic calculation on spheroid (GeographicLib). YB: point-to-point only, Haversine on sphere (2-arg) or Vincenty on ellipsoid (3-arg).

it picks only the first entry of the shape and calculate the point only
In Plain English: What is the shortest straight-line distance between shape A and shape B?

Can we support it: C library
In Plain English: If I travel 50% down this line, where exactly am I?

Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Returns the fraction (0..1) of the closest point on the line. (so there will be a T)
PostGIS Gaps: Accepts the preserveCollapsed param but ignores it, delegating to the 2-arg version.
Can we support it: C library
In Plain English: What percentage of the way down this line is closest to my location?

Function name (including params): ST_LineSubstring(p_geom geometry, p_start_frac double precision, p_end_frac double precision)
Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Extracts a sub-line between two fractions.
PostGIS Gaps: Accepts the preserveCollapsed param but ignores it, delegating to the 2-arg version.
Can we support it: C library
In Plain English: Cut a piece out of this line starting at 10% and ending at 90%.

Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Parses WKT strings. Supports POINT, LINESTRING, POLYGON (no holes, no multi-geometries).
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS parses all WKT types (Multi*, GeometryCollection, curves, 3D). YB: POINT/LINESTRING/POLYGON only, no multi-geometry, no holes, no 3D.
Can we support it: Yes
In Plain English: Read a text string like "POINT(-111 40)" and turn it into a database shape.

Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Parses GeoJSON Point, LineString, Polygon. Same thing as above but parse JSON
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS parses all GeoJSON types including Multi*, GeometryCollection, 3D; accepts text/json/jsonb. YB: Point/LineString/Polygon only, no multi-geometry, text input only.
Can we support it: Yes
In Plain English: Read a JSON object and turn it into a database shape.

Function name (including params): ST_Rotate(p_geom geometry, p_angle double precision, p_cx double precision DEFAULT 0, p_cy double precision DEFAULT 0)
Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: 2D rotation around a center point.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS rotates complex multi-geometries and holes. YB: rotates a single 2D array only.
Can we support it: Possible, by building a C-extension geometry type. A proper struct allows iterating over all parts of a MultiGeometry/hole hierarchy.
In Plain English: Spin this shape around a center point.

Function name (including params): ST_Affine(p_geom geometry, p_a double precision, p_b double precision, p_d double precision, p_e double precision, p_xoff double precision, p_yoff double precision)
Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: General 2D affine transform. x' = a*x + b*y + xoff; y' = d*x + e*y + yoff
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS supports full 3D affine transformations and applies them across complex multi-geometries. YB: 2D only, simple single array.
Can we support it: Possible, by building a C-extension geometry type. A proper struct allows iterating over all parts of a complex shape, and adding 3D math parameters.
In Plain English: Apply complex math to shift, scale, and rotate this shape all at once.

Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Returns each vertex as `(path integer[], geom geometry)`.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS returns a path array that explicitly tells you where the vertex came from (e.g., Polygon 1, Inner Ring 2, Vertex 5). YB: The path is just [1], [2] because there is no hierarchy to report.
Can we support it: Possible, by building a C-extension geometry type. A proper GSERIALIZED struct tracks the hierarchy of rings and collections, allowing the dump function to populate the full path array correctly.
In Plain English: Extract every single point out of a complex shape and return them as individual rows.

Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Returns each edge as `(path integer[], geom geometry)`.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS returns a path array detailing the source of the segment within complex MultiGeometries. YB: The path is just [1], [2] because there is no hierarchy to report.
Can we support it: Possible, by building a C-extension geometry type. A proper GSERIALIZED struct tracks the hierarchy, allowing the dump function to populate the full path array correctly.
In Plain English: Extract every single line segment out of a complex shape and return them as individual rows.

Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Rounds coordinates to nearest grid multiple.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS allows snapping Z/M coordinates to different grid sizes, supports origin offsets, and handles multi-geometries. YB: 2D X/Y snapping only, no origin offset, single array.
Can we support it: Possible, by building a C-extension geometry type. A proper struct allows iterating over all parts of a complex shape and supports Z/M coordinates for full multi-dimensional grid snapping.
In Plain English: Round all the coordinates so they snap neatly to a grid.

Function name (including params): ST_RemoveRepeatedPoints(p_geom geometry, p_tolerance double precision DEFAULT 0)
Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Removes consecutive duplicate vertices within tolerance.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS applies this recursively to every ring inside every polygon of a MultiPolygon, and includes 3D distances. YB: Applies the 2D algorithm to a single array only.
Can we support it: Possible, by building a C-extension geometry type. A proper struct allows iterating over and editing all rings within a complex geometry hierarchy, while incorporating 3D distance math.
In Plain English: Delete back-to-back duplicate coordinates to clean up the data.

Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Subdivides long segments by inserting intermediate vertices. (divided by length)
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS applies this recursively to every ring inside every polygon of a MultiPolygon, and includes 3D distances. YB: Applies the 2D algorithm to a single array only.
Can we support it: Possible, by building a C-extension geometry type. A proper struct allows iterating over and editing all rings within a complex geometry hierarchy, while incorporating 3D distance math.
In Plain English: Chop long lines into smaller segments so no piece is longer than X miles.

Function name (including params): ST_ClipByBox2D(p_geom geometry, p_xmin double precision, p_ymin double precision, p_xmax double precision, p_ymax double precision)
Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Clips polygon to axis-aligned bounding box using Sutherland-Hodgman. It returns the overlap of two shape.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS clips any geometry type against a box2d. YB: single polygon only, no multi-geometry.
In Plain English: Cut this shape so only the part inside this rectangle remains.

Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Clips polygon to axis-aligned bounding box using Sutherland-Hodgman. It returns the overlap of two shape.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS clips any geometry type against a box2d. YB: single polygon only, no multi-geometry.
In Plain English: Cut this shape so only the part inside this rectangle remains.

Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Random points inside a polygon via rejection sampling.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS returns MultiPoint; has seed param (since 3.0) for deterministic results; supports MultiPolygon input. YB: returns SETOF geometry (individual points), no seed param, single polygon only.
In Plain English: Randomly scatter 50 points inside this polygon.

Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Corner-cutting smoothing.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS has preserveEndPoints param (default false), max 5 iterations, handles 3D/M, proper ring closure for polygons. YB: missing preserveEndPoints, no iteration cap, no ring closure handling, 2D only.
In Plain English: Round off the sharp corners of this shape to make it smooth.

Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Expands the bounding box by `amount` in all 4 directions.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS supports expanding a 3D/4D bounding box (adding dz and dm parameters). YB: expands a 2D bounding box only.
Can we support it: Possible, by building a C-extension geometry type. A proper struct explicitly supports Z and M dimensions, allowing the expansion logic to apply across all axes.
In Plain English: Make this bounding box slightly larger in all directions.

Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Text description: geometry type, vertex count, bounding box.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS provides summary strings for all complex types (MultiPolygons, GeometryCollections) and explicitly flags Z/M dimensions. YB: only reports on 3 basic types (Point, LineString, Polygon) in 2D.
Can we support it: Possible, by building a C-extension geometry type. A proper struct includes header bytes identifying the exact geometry type and dimensional flags, allowing the summary function to accurately describe complex shapes.
In Plain English: Give me a quick text summary of what this shape is and how big its bounding box is.

Function name (including params): ST_AddPoint(p_geom geometry, p_point geometry, p_position integer DEFAULT -1)
Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Vertex editing (0-based index).
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS explicitly restricts this to LineStrings (fails on Polygons/Points) and correctly splices 3D/M coordinates. YB: blindly splices the 2D array without type-checking.
Can we support it: Possible, by building a C-extension geometry type. A proper struct allows strict type validation (rejecting non-LineStrings) and handles multi-dimensional coordinate splicing.
In Plain English: Insert a new coordinate into this line.

Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Vertex editing (0-based index).
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS explicitly restricts this to LineStrings and safely removes multi-dimensional coordinates. YB: blindly splices the 2D array without type-checking.
Can we support it: Possible, by building a C-extension geometry type. A proper struct allows strict type validation (rejecting non-LineStrings) and handles multi-dimensional coordinate removal.
In Plain English: Delete a specific coordinate from this line.

Function name (including params): ST_SetPoint(p_geom geometry, p_index integer, p_point geometry)
Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Vertex editing (0-based index).
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS explicitly restricts this to LineStrings and safely replaces multi-dimensional coordinates. YB: blindly overwrites the 2D array index without type-checking.
Can we support it: Possible, by building a C-extension geometry type. A proper struct allows strict type validation (rejecting non-LineStrings) and handles multi-dimensional coordinate replacement.
In Plain English: Replace a specific coordinate in this line with a new one.

Function name (including params): ST_Project(p_point geometry, p_distance double precision, -- meters p_azimuth double precision -- radians)
Section: Section 7: Tier2_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 2 geometry functions
Note: Forward geodesic projection using Vincenty direct formula on WGS84.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS geometry ST_Project uses GeographicLib on the ellipsoid. YB: spherical approximation (R=6371km), not ellipsoidal.
In Plain English: If I start here, face a specific direction, and walk 500 feet, where do I end up?

Section: Section 8: Tier3_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 3 geometry functions
Note: Graham scan convex hull. (smallest convex polygon that encloses all the points.) Convex is a polygon that has no corner that’s \>= 180 degree
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS uses GEOS; handles any geometry type including Multi* and GeometryCollection; returns Point/LineString for degenerate cases. YB: correct Graham scan but no multi-geometry, O(n^2) sort.
In Plain English: Wrap a tight rubber band around all these points and give me that shape.

Section: Section 8: Tier3_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 3 geometry functions
Note: Sutherland-Hodgman polygon clipping. Exact for convex polygons.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS uses GEOS for exact boolean intersection on any geometry type; supports gridSize param. YB: Sutherland-Hodgman — exact for convex polygons only, approximate for non-convex.
In Plain English: Give me the new shape created by the exact overlapping area of shape A and shape B.

Section: Section 8: Tier3_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 3 geometry functions
Note: Convex hull of combined vertices. Approximate for non-convex shapes.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS uses GEOS for exact boolean union on any geometry type; supports gridSize param and aggregate form. YB: returns convex hull of combined vertices — not a true union.
In Plain English: Melt shape A and shape B together into one giant shape.

Section: Section 8: Tier3_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 3 geometry functions
Note: Vertices of A outside B plus intersection points along edges. Exact for convex shapes.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS uses GEOS for exact boolean difference on any geometry type; supports gridSize param. YB: vertex-based approach — exact for convex polygon pairs only.
In Plain English: Cut shape B out of shape A, and give me what is left over.

Section: Section 8: Tier3_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 3 geometry functions
Note: Convex hull of (A\B union B\A). Approximate. ST_Difference(A, B) returns the part of A that is NOT inside B
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS uses GEOS for exact symmetric difference on any geometry type; supports gridSize param. YB: returns convex hull of the two differences — not a true symmetric difference.
In Plain English: Give me the parts of shape A and shape B that DO NOT overlap.

Function name (including params): ST_Buffer(p_geom geometry, p_distance double precision, p_segments integer DEFAULT 8)
Section: Section 8: Tier3_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 3 geometry functions
Note: Point: generates circular polygon. Polygon: offsets vertices along bisector normals. Approximate.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS uses GEOS for precise buffer with configurable end cap/join styles (round/flat/mitre) and quad_segs. YB: point=circle approximation, polygon=rough vertex offset along bisector normals, no arc segments at corners, no style options.
In Plain English: Draw a border exactly 5 miles outward around this entire shape.

Section: Section 8: Tier3_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 3 geometry functions
Note: Checks for empty arrays, NaN/Inf coordinates, self-intersecting edges, and non-zero area for polygons.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS uses GEOS for full OGC validity (ring orientation, nesting, duplicate rings, etc.); supports flags param for ESRI mode. YB: basic checks only (empty/NaN/self-intersection/zero-area), no flags param.
In Plain English: Check if this shape is mathematically sound (e.g., lines don't cross themselves).

Section: Section 8: Tier3_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 3 geometry functions
Note: True if boundaries contact but interiors do not overlap.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS uses GEOS DE-9IM (patterns FT*******, F**T*****, F***T****); handles all geometry types. YB: simplified boundary-distance check, no full DE-9IM.
In Plain English: Do the borders of these two shapes touch without overlapping?

Section: Section 8: Tier3_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 3 geometry functions
Note: True if geometries have partial interior intersection at lower dimensions.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS uses GEOS DE-9IM; handles all dimension combinations per OGC spec. YB: simplified — only handles line-vs-polygon and line-vs-line cases.
In Plain English: Does this line slice completely through this polygon?

Section: Section 8: Tier3_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 3 geometry functions
Note: True if same-dimension geometries partially overlap and neither contains the other.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS uses GEOS DE-9IM; handles all same-dimension geometry pairs. YB: simplified — checks intersects + neither-contains, no full DE-9IM.
In Plain English: Do these two polygons partially cover each other like a Venn diagram?

Section: Section 8: Tier3_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 3 geometry functions
Note: Topological equality. For polygons: mutual containment. For lines/points: same vertices in any order.
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS uses GEOS DE-9IM for topological equality; handles all geometry types. YB: mutual ST_Contains for polygons, vertex-by-vertex for lines. No multi-geometry.
In Plain English: Are these two shapes physically identical in space?

Section: Section 8: Tier3_GeometryFunctions.sql - Define the tier 3 geometry functions
Note: Visvalingam-Whyatt algorithm. Removes vertices by effective triangle area.(remove dots that doesn’t change the polygon)
PostGIS Gaps: PostGIS Visvalingam-Whyatt handles all geometry types; supports 3D. YB: correct VW but no multi-geometry, no 3D.
In Plain English: Remove visually insignificant corners to make the shape smaller.

Section: Section 9: GeohashPolygonFunctions.sql - Define the geohash polygon functions
Note: Given 3-5 geohash strings that define polygon vertices, returns all precision-8 cells fully contained within it.
In Plain English: Find all the tiny Geohash squares that fit completely inside this large shape.

Note: Enumerates all geohash cells covering a bounding box at the given precision. Core function for the two-phase query pattern.
In Plain English: Give me a list of all the Geohash squares that cover this large rectangle.
